## zerops
ZCP manages Zerops PaaS infrastructure through workflows.

Primary entry â€” every user task that changes code or deploys:
  zerops_workflow action="start" workflow="develop" intent="<one-liner>"

The develop workflow carries step-by-step guidance, tracks deploys and
verifies, and auto-closes when scoped services are green.

Other entries (when they fit):
  workflow="bootstrap"  â€” create/adopt infrastructure (empty project, add a service, mode expansion)
  workflow="export"     â€” turn a deployed service into a re-importable git repo

Recipe authoring uses zerops_recipe (v3 engine, separate tool). Entry:
  zerops_recipe action="start" slug="<slug>" outputRoot="<dir>"

Deploy configuration:
  action="strategy" strategies={hostname:"push-dev|push-git|manual"}
    â€” central point for deploy config; push-git returns full setup flow
      (GIT_TOKEN, optional CI/CD via GitHub Actions or webhook, first push)

Direct tools â€” read-only queries and config tweaks that don't need a
deploy cycle: zerops_discover, zerops_logs, zerops_events,
zerops_knowledge, zerops_env, zerops_manage, zerops_scale,
zerops_subdomain, zerops_verify.

Workflow-gated: zerops_deploy (adopted services), zerops_mount and
zerops_import (active workflow session).

Recovery â€” after compaction or when the state is unclear:
  zerops_workflow action="status"
Running as the ZCP control-plane container â€” Ubuntu with zcli, psql, mysql,
redis-cli, jq, and network to every service. App code lives in the runtime
containers (reach via ssh {hostname} "..."), not here. Files at
/var/www/{hostname}/ are SSHFS mounts â€” edit with Read/Edit/Write, not SSH.
CLI helpers like jq/psql are missing inside runtimes; pipe output back to
ZCP. Edits on the mount survive restart but not deploy. zerops_discover
refreshes service state.
You are running on 'zcp'. Other services in this project are yours to manage.

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring or one-time remote agent ("run this every Monday", "open a cleanup PR for X in 2 weeks"), or to manage existing routines. ALSO OFFER PROACTIVELY: after you finish work that has a natural future follow-up, end your reply with a one-line offer to schedule a background agent to do it. Strong signals: a feature flag / gate / experiment / staged rollout was just shipped (offer a one-time agent in ~2 weeks to open a cleanup PR or evaluate results), a new alert/monitor was created (offer a recurring agent to triage it), a TODO/migration with a "remove once X" condition was left behind (offer a one-time agent to do the removal). Skip the offer for refactors, bug fixes, and anything that is done once it ships. Name a concrete action and cadence ("in 2 weeks", "every Monday") and only offer when the run just succeeded â€” do not pitch a schedule for something that has not happened yet.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 â†’ 4.6, 4.6 â†’ 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

## Canonical services â€” authoritative versions

Do not guess versions. Use these exactly. Do not add services not in
this table.

| Kind    | Hostname  | Type             |
|---------|-----------|------------------|
| db      | `db`      | `postgresql@18`  |
| cache   | `cache`   | `valkey@7.2`     |
| broker  | `broker`  | `nats@2.12`      |
| storage | `storage` | `object-storage` |
| search  | `search`  | `meilisearch@1.20` |

Mail / SMTP / Mailpit are NOT canonical showcase services. Production
users bring their own SMTP. The research gate rejects non-canonical
hostnames at `complete-phase`.

## Runtime types

Match the framework family: `nodejs@22` / `php-nginx@8.4` / `python@3.14`
/ `go@1` / `rust@stable` / `java@21` / `dotnet@9` / `ruby@3.4` /
`bun@1.2` / `deno@2` / `elixir@1.16`. Pick the latest from the family
your framework uses.

## Service set per tier

- `hello-world-{lang}` â†’ runtime only, **0** managed services.
- `{framework}-minimal` â†’ runtime + `db` (1 managed service).
- `{framework}-showcase` â†’ runtime + all **5** canonical services above.

## Classification â€” full-stack vs API-first

Apply to pick codebase shape. Use your framework knowledge:

- **Full-stack** (built-in view engine â€” Laravel/Blade, Rails/ERB,
  Django/Jinja2, Phoenix/HEEx, SvelteKit+server, Next.js+server):
  â†’ **shape 1** (monolith). One codebase, `role=monolith`.
- **API-first** (JSON-only â€” NestJS, Express, Fastify, Hono, FastAPI,
  Flask API, Spring Boot, Gin, Axum, Actix): â†’ **shape 2 or 3**.
  - Shape 2: 2 codebases. `role=api` + `role=frontend`. Worker shares
    api's codebase (queue library runs as sibling process).
  - Shape 3: 3 codebases. Same two + separate worker codebase.
    Use when the framework's worker needs a first-class long-lived
    context distinct from the API (NestJS `createApplicationContext`,
    Express standalone worker). For NestJS specifically: shape 3.

hello-world and minimal tiers are always shape 1 regardless of
framework family â€” they test runtime + platform, not service fan-out.

## Frontend default (shape 2/3 only)

Svelte + Vite compiled to static. Deploys on `static` runtime in prod.
Build: `npm ci && npm run build`. Don't pick React/Vue/Angular unless
the user asked for one by name.

## Parent recipe handling

The `parent` field in the `start` response tells you whether the chain
resolver found a published predecessor:

- **`parent` populated**: read `parent.codebases[].readme` and
  `parent.envImports["0"]` verbatim. Inherit hostnames, runtime type,
  and services â€” don't re-derive. Add only showcase-new services.
- **`parent` is null**: first-time run for this framework (or parent
  not mounted at `~/recipes/`). Proceed from your framework training
  + this atom. **Do not call `zerops_knowledge` to substitute for the
  missing parent** â€” the service set and versions above are
  authoritative whether or not parent exists.

## Payload shape for update-plan

```json
{
  "framework": "<slug root, e.g. \"nestjs\">",
  "tier": "hello-world | minimal | showcase",
  "research": {
    "codebaseShape": "1 | 2 | 3",
    "needsAppSecret": true,
    "appSecretKey": "<env-var name, e.g. APP_SECRET / APP_KEY>",
    "description": "<one sentence>"
  },
  "codebases": [
    {"hostname": "api",    "role": "api",      "baseRuntime": "nodejs@22"},
    {"hostname": "app",    "role": "frontend", "baseRuntime": "nodejs@22"},
    {"hostname": "worker", "role": "worker",   "baseRuntime": "nodejs@22",
     "isWorker": true, "sharesCodebaseWith": ""}
  ],
  "services": [
    {"hostname": "db",      "type": "postgresql@18",    "kind": "managed", "priority": 10},
    {"hostname": "cache",   "type": "valkey@7.2",       "kind": "managed", "priority": 10},
    {"hostname": "broker",  "type": "nats@2.12",        "kind": "managed", "priority": 10},
    {"hostname": "storage", "type": "object-storage",   "kind": "storage"},
    {"hostname": "search",  "type": "meilisearch@1.20", "kind": "managed", "priority": 10}
  ]
}
```

Above example is a NestJS showcase (shape 3). Swap framework/tier/
codebases/roles for other combinations.

## Then

1. `zerops_recipe action=update-plan slug=<slug> plan=<payload>` (merges
   into session â€” you can send partials)
2. `zerops_recipe action=complete-phase slug=<slug>` â†’ runs the research
   gate. Read `violations` on failure, patch via another update-plan,
   retry complete-phase. Do NOT call `zerops_knowledge` to understand
   violations â€” the violation message itself names the field + fix.
"}

{"ok":true,"action":"complete-phase","slug":"nestjs-showcase","status":{"slug":"nestjs-showcase","current":"research","completed":["research"],"codebases":3,"services":5,"factsCount":0},"guidance":"Next phase: provision

# Provision phase â€” bring up the working project

Provision creates the single Zerops project this recipe run iterates
against. Scaffold + feature phases deploy code into it; finalize
generates the 6 published tier yamls separately â€” **provision does not
create any of those 6 tiers as live projects**. It creates one workspace.

## The two distinct YAML shapes â€” do not conflate

- **Workspace YAML** (this phase): services-only, no `project:` block,
  dev runtimes `startWithoutCode: true` so they come up empty for you
  to write code into via SSH/mount, stage runtimes wait at
  `READY_TO_DEPLOY`, no `buildFromGit` (repos don't exist yet), no
  `zeropsSetup`, no preprocessor expressions. Submitted inline via
  `zerops_import content=<yaml>`.

- **Deliverable YAMLs** (6 files, produced at finalize): full `project:`
  block per tier with `envVariables`, every runtime has
  `zeropsSetup: dev|prod` + `buildFromGit` pointing at the published
  codebase repos, shared secrets use `<@generateRandomString(<32>)>`
  templates so every end-user's click-deploy gets a fresh value.

The workspace yaml you submit here is NOT one of the 6 deliverables. Do
not try to pass a deliverable yaml to `zerops_import` â€” the repos don't
exist yet and it would fail at the clone step.

## Steps

1. **Emit the workspace yaml**:
   `zerops_recipe action=emit-yaml slug=<slug> shape=workspace`

   Returns services-only yaml with dev+stage pairs per codebase + all
   managed services. No disk write â€” the yaml string is the response.

2. **Provision the workspace**:
   `zerops_import content=<yaml from step 1>` (pass the string inline,
   do not write it to disk first). Wait for every service to reach its
   expected state:
   - Dev runtimes â†’ `RUNNING` (via `startWithoutCode: true`)
   - Stage runtimes â†’ `READY_TO_DEPLOY` (wait for first cross-deploy)
   - Managed services â†’ `RUNNING` / `ACTIVE`

3. **Set project-level shared secrets** (if `Research.NeedsAppSecret=true`):
   ```
   zerops_env project=true action=set \
     variables=["<AppSecretKey>=<@generateRandomString(<32>)>"]
   ```
   The preprocessor runs once, the actual secret value lands on the
   live project, and dependent services restart with it. **This is the
   real secret your workspace uses.** The 6 deliverable yamls emit their
   own `<@generateRandomString>` template at finalize â€” each end-user's
   click-deploy gets a different value, which is correct.

4. **Mount dev codebases** (one per non-worker-shared codebase):
   `zerops_mount serviceHostname=<codebase>dev`. SSHFS mounts land on
   the `startWithoutCode` dev containers.

5. **Catalog cross-service env var keys**:
   `zerops_discover includeEnvs=true`. Record the authoritative env-var
   keys each managed service exposes â€” `${db_hostname}`, `${db_user}`,
   `${cache_hostname}`, etc. Scaffold sub-agents reference these in each
   codebase's `zerops.yaml run.envVariables`, never raw values.

6. **Complete the phase**:
   `zerops_recipe action=complete-phase slug=<slug>`.

## What NOT to do

- Do NOT emit a deliverable yaml at provision. Deliverable shape has
  `buildFromGit` pointing at repos that don't exist yet.
- Do NOT write the workspace yaml to disk. `zerops_import` takes
  `content` inline.
- Do NOT declare shared secrets in the workspace yaml's `envVariables`
  (there is no `project:` block in workspace shape). Use `zerops_env
  project=true action=set` after import.
- Do NOT bake your workspace's real secret value into anything that
  flows to finalize. Finalize emits `<@generateRandomString>` templates
  for reproducibility.
- Do NOT call `zerops_import` with a hand-written yaml. Use the
  engine-emitted workspace shape. If the emitter produces invalid yaml,
  record a fact and fix the emitter via PR.
"}

# Scaffold phase â€” one sub-agent dispatch per codebase

Every codebase in `plan.codebases` gets ONE scaffold sub-agent dispatch.
The sub-agent writes source code + `zerops.yaml` for its codebase; the
main agent coordinates.

## For each codebase (parallelize when shape is 2 or 3)

1. **Compose brief**:
   `zerops_recipe action=build-brief slug=<slug>
   briefKind=scaffold codebase=<hostname>`

   The brief returns platform-obligation prose (bind 0.0.0.0, trust
   proxy, execOnce for migrations, SIGTERM drain) plus the parent
   codebase's README excerpt if the chain resolver found one. It does
   NOT contain framework-specific instructions â€” the sub-agent brings
   that from its own knowledge.

2. **Dispatch the sub-agent** via the `Agent` tool. Pass the brief's
   `body` verbatim as the `prompt`. Description: `scaffold-<hostname>`.

3. **Sub-agent produces**: source tree under the Zerops service's
   SSHFS mount (`/var/www/<hostname>/` in-container, or equivalent
   local path), including `zerops.yaml`. It deploys to its service
   (`zerops_deploy targetService=<hostname>`) and runs the preship
   contract from its brief (HTTP reachable, X-Forwarded-For echoes,
   SIGTERM drain, migrations ran). Records facts for any deviation.

4. **Verify the deploy**: `zerops_verify targetService=<hostname>`.
   Every scaffold codebase must be green before the phase completes.

## Dispatch integrity

The engine builds the brief; the main agent dispatches it. If the main
agent paraphrases or truncates the brief before dispatch, the sub-agent
misses critical platform rules. Always pass `brief.body` byte-identical.

`zerops_recipe action=verify-subagent-dispatch` is planned but not yet
implemented in v3 â€” for now, do not paraphrase.

## Complete-phase gate

Every plan.codebase hostname must have a deployed + verified service.
Scaffold facts recorded during the phase flow into the writer brief at
finalize â€” more facts = richer content. Under-record is visible; over-
record is not.
"}

{"ok":true,"action":"build-brief","slug":"nestjs-showcase","brief":{"kind":"scaffold","body":"# Scaffold brief â€” api (api)

Recipe: nestjs-showcase Â· Framework: nestjs Â· Tier: showcase

## Role contract
- ServesHTTP: true
- RequiresSubdomain: true
- ProcessModel: http-server
- zeropsSetup dev: dev, prod: prod

# Platform obligations

## HTTP surface (ServesHTTP=true)

- Bind `0.0.0.0`, read `PORT` env var â€” loopback is unreachable.
- Trust `X-Forwarded-*` (the L7 balancer sets them).
- `zerops.yaml run.ports`: `{ port: <PORT>, httpSupport: true }`.

## Managed services

- Cross-service env vars auto-inject project-wide. Do NOT declare
  `DB_HOST: ${db_hostname}` â€” the platform copy and the alias collide
  and blank at container start (self-shadow). Cite `env-var-model`.
- Valkey: auth-free. Postgres: `{hostname}_{user,password,hostname,port}`.

## Migrations / one-time setup

- `initCommands`: `zsc execOnce <static-key> -- <cmd>`. Pair with
  `--retryUntilSuccessful`. Cite `execOnce`.
- execOnce marks the key successful on exit 0 â€” silent no-ops burn it.

## Rolling deploys

- Accept `SIGTERM`, drain, exit. Zerops sends it before removing the
  container from the balancer.
- `minContainers: 2` at tier 4+ â€” two replicas run simultaneously.

# Pre-ship contract

Verify behavior, not code. Preship passes when all five are green on
the dev container.

1. `curl ${appdev-subdomain}/health` â†’ 200 + body.
2. `curl -H "X-Forwarded-For: 1.2.3.4" /debug/remote-ip` echoes
   `1.2.3.4`. Remove `/debug/remote-ip` before prod tiers.
3. Restart the service while a 2s+ request is in flight â€” request
   finishes 200, new requests hit the new container.
4. Migrations attested once post-deploy; second deploy does not re-run.
5. stderr in first 10s post-boot is empty of error-level logs.

Record a fact for any deviation.

# Fact recording

Schema (all required):

- `topic` â€” short kebab-case
- `symptom` â€” observable failure (status + quoted error)
- `mechanism` â€” why (platform-side; both sides if intersection)
- `surface_hint` â€” one of: `root-overview`, `tier-promotion`,
  `tier-decision`, `porter-change`, `platform-trap`, `operational`,
  `scaffold-decision`
- `citation` â€” `zerops_knowledge` guide id or published-recipe URL.
  Required for every `platform-trap` / `porter-change` fact.

Self-inflicted findings (code bugs fixed during scaffold) are NOT
facts â€” discard.

","bytes":2325,"parts":["header","role_contract","briefs/scaffold/platform_principles.md","briefs/scaffold/preship_contract.md","briefs/scaffold/fact_recording.md"]}}

input.prompt:
You are the scaffold sub-agent for the **api** codebase in recipe `nestjs-showcase`.

## Concrete deliverables

- **Framework**: NestJS 11 (TypeScript), Express adapter, `@nestjs/config`, `@nestjs/typeorm`, `typeorm`, `pg`, `ioredis`, `nats`, `@aws-sdk/client-s3`, `meilisearch`, `class-validator`, `class-transformer`.
- **Domain**: `notes` resource â€” `id`, `title`, `body`, `createdAt`.
  - `POST /notes` â†’ create, publish `notes.created` on NATS, index in Meilisearch.
  - `GET /notes` â†’ list (TypeORM find).
  - `GET /notes/:id` â†’ one, cache via Valkey for 30s.
  - `DELETE /notes/:id` â†’ delete, publish `notes.deleted`, remove from index.
  - `GET /notes/search?q=` â†’ Meilisearch query against `notes` index.
  - `POST /uploads` (multipart `file`) â†’ push to object-storage bucket, return signed URL.
  - `GET /health` â†’ `{ status: 'ok' }` 200.
  - `GET /debug/remote-ip` â†’ echoes `X-Forwarded-For` first hop (REMOVE before the prod tier â€” leave a `// TODO: remove in prod` marker).
- **Migrations**: TypeORM migration that creates the `notes` table. Run via `zsc execOnce notes-init-v1 --retryUntilSuccessful -- npm run migration:run`.
- **Boot**: listen on `0.0.0.0:${PORT}`; trust proxy (`app.set('trust proxy', true)`); handle `SIGTERM` via `app.enableShutdownHooks()`.

## Working directory

Write all source under `/var/www/apidev/` (SSHFS mount of the apidev runtime). You have Read/Edit/Write and Bash. Use `ssh apidev "..."` to run commands inside the runtime container if you need node/npm (node 22 is installed there). Do not run `npm install` on the ZCP container â€” run it inside apidev: `ssh apidev "cd ~/work && npm install"` â€” or better, let the deploy `build.buildCommands` do it so it lands in the build image.

## Env vars â€” reference at runtime only

Cross-service env vars are auto-injected project-wide. Read them via `process.env.db_hostname`, `process.env.db_user`, `process.env.db_password`, `process.env.db_dbName`, `process.env.db_port`, `process.env.cache_hostname`, `process.env.cache_port`, `process.env.broker_hostname`, `process.env.broker_port`, `process.env.broker_user`, `process.env.broker_password`, `process.env.search_hostname`, `process.env.search_port`, `process.env.search_masterKey`, `process.env.storage_accessKeyId`, `process.env.storage_secretAccessKey`, `process.env.storage_apiHost`, `process.env.storage_bucketName`, `process.env.APP_SECRET`.

DO NOT alias these in `zerops.yaml run.envVariables` (e.g. `DB_HOST: ${db_hostname}` self-shadows â€” see brief). Reference them by their native keys in code, or derive non-colliding names (`POSTGRES_URL`, `REDIS_URL`, `NATS_URL`, `S3_ENDPOINT`, `MEILI_URL`).

`PORT`: provided by Zerops at runtime, your app must read it.

## zerops.yaml contract

```
zerops:
  - setup: api
    build:
      base: nodejs@22
      buildCommands:
        - npm ci
        - npm run build
      deployFiles:
        - dist
        - node_modules
        - package.json
        - package-lock.json
    run:
      base: nodejs@22
      ports:
        - port: 3000
          httpSupport: true
      envVariables:
        PORT: '3000'
        NODE_ENV: production
        # POSTGRES_URL etc. may be derived here using ${db_connectionString} references â€” safe because name differs from platform key
        POSTGRES_URL: postgres://${db_user}:${db_password}@${db_hostname}:${db_port}/${db_dbName}
        REDIS_URL: redis://${cache_hostname}:${cache_port}
        NATS_URL: nats://${broker_user}:${broker_password}@${broker_hostname}:${broker_port}
        MEILI_URL: http://${search_hostname}:${search_port}
        MEILI_KEY: ${search_masterKey}
        S3_ENDPOINT: http://${storage_apiHost}
        S3_BUCKET: ${storage_bucketName}
        S3_ACCESS_KEY: ${storage_accessKeyId}
        S3_SECRET_KEY: ${storage_secretAccessKey}
      initCommands:
        - zsc execOnce notes-init-v1 --retryUntilSuccessful -- node dist/migrations/run.js
      start: node dist/main.js
```

Adjust as needed (e.g. TypeORM migration runner path).

## Deploy + verify

After you write code and `zerops.yaml`:

1. Commit nothing (no git) â€” deploy directly: `mcp__zerops__zerops_deploy targetService=apidev workingDir=/var/www/apidev setup=api`.
2. Run `mcp__zerops__zerops_verify targetService=apidev`.
3. Exercise the preship contract against the apidev subdomain (get it via `mcp__zerops__zerops_subdomain action=get serviceHostname=apidev` â€” enable if needed). Curl `/health`, `/debug/remote-ip` with X-Forwarded-For, POST a note, GET list.
4. If anything fails, fix it on the mount and redeploy.

Tools you can call: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, ToolSearch, and all `mcp__zerops__*` tools. For Zerops MCP tools you haven't loaded yet, use `ToolSearch query="select:mcp__zerops__zerops_deploy,mcp__zerops__zerops_verify,mcp__zerops__zerops_subdomain,mcp__zerops__zerops_logs,mcp__zerops__zerops_events,mcp__zerops__zerops_record_fact"` first.

Record facts via `mcp__zerops__zerops_record_fact` for any genuine platform surprise (not self-inflicted code bugs).

Report back: green preship, any facts recorded, and a short note of what's deployed.

<result>All endpoints green (the syntax error is in my python oneliner, not the API). The upload curl succeeded (it started streaming the response to python which then crashed), verified by earlier test. 

Final state summary:

**Deployed**: nestjs-showcase api on `apidev` at `https://apidev-21ec-3000.prg1.zerops.app`.

**Preship contract verified**:
- `/health` â†’ `{"status":"ok"}` HTTP 200
- `/debug/remote-ip` with `X-Forwarded-For: 9.8.7.6` â†’ `firstHop: "9.8.7.6"`, `remoteIp: "9.8.7.6"` (trust proxy + XFF first-hop parsing)
- `POST /notes` â†’ 201 with persisted note, event published on `notes.created`, indexed in Meilisearch
- `GET /notes` â†’ list sorted by createdAt desc
- `GET /notes/:id` â†’ cached via Valkey for 30s
- `DELETE /notes/:id` â†’ deletes row, cache key, publishes `notes.deleted`, removes from index
- `GET /notes/search?q=` â†’ Meilisearch query returns hits after indexing
- `POST /uploads` â†’ 201 with bucket key + working 5-min signed URL
- Migration ran once via `zsc execOnce notes-init-v1` on first deploy; subsequent deploys no-op (execOnce lock held)
- Zero ERROR logs in first 10s post-boot on final deploy
- `app.enableShutdownHooks()` installed for SIGTERM drain
- `// TODO: remove in prod` markers on `DebugController` class + `@Get('remote-ip')` method

**Source location**: Canonical source lives at `/home/zerops/apidev-src/` on ZCP (because SSHFS mount `/var/www/apidev/` is overwritten with only `deployFiles` after every deploy â€” source must be re-synced before each deploy via `rsync -a /home/zerops/apidev-src/ /var/www/apidev/ --exclude node_modules --exclude dist`).

**Key files**:
- `/home/zerops/apidev-src/src/main.ts` â€” bootstrap, trust proxy, enableShutdownHooks
- `/home/zerops/apidev-src/src/app.module.ts` â€” TypeORM postgres DS via `process.env.db_*`
- `/home/zerops/apidev-src/src/notes/` â€” entity, dto, controller, service (business fan-out)
- `/home/zerops/apidev-src/src/infra/` â€” cache (ioredis), broker (nats), search (meilisearch), storage (S3 + presign)
- `/home/zerops/apidev-src/src/uploads/uploads.controller.ts` â€” multipart â†’ S3 â†’ signed URL
- `/home/zerops/apidev-src/src/migrations/` â€” TypeORM standalone migration runner + CreateNotesTable
- `/home/zerops/apidev-src/zerops.yaml` â€” setup: `api`, PORT 3000, derived URL aliases, `execOnce` init

**Facts discovered (record via parent recipe session â€” my sub-agent context had no active session so `zerops_record_fact` rejected)**:

1. **nats-js v2 rejects credentials in the servers URL**. `connect({ servers: 'nats://user:pass@host:4222' })` throws `Invalid URL` and never connects. The client requires the separate `user`/`pass` options. Zerops exposes `broker_user`/`broker_password`/`broker_hostname`/`broker_port` as discrete keys, so the natural `NATS_URL` composition via `${broker_user}:${broker_password}@...` is a trap. Evidence: runtime log `[BrokerService] nats connect failed: Invalid URL`; minimal repro confirmed. Fix: `connect({ servers: \`nats://${host}:${port}\`, user: broker_user, pass: broker_password })`. Surface hint: `platform-trap`. Suggested route: `content_gotcha`.

2. **Zerops object-storage `apiHost` returns 301 over http://**. Using `http://${storage_apiHost}` as the S3 endpoint lands the AWS SDK v3 on an nginx 301 HTML redirect to https. The SDK does not follow cross-scheme redirects and tries to parse the HTML body as S3 XML, producing `Error: Expected closing tag 'hr' (opened in line 5, col 1) instead of closing tag 'body'` and HTTP 500 on `/uploads`. Evidence: `curl -I http://storage-prg1.zerops.io/` â†’ `301 Moved Permanently` + `Location: https://storage-prg1.zerops.io/`. Fix: use `https://${storage_apiHost}` (or read `storage_apiUrl` directly, which is already https). Surface hint: `platform-trap`. Suggested route: `content_gotcha`.

**Dependency pin note (framework-side, not a platform fact)**: `@nestjs/config@^3.x` peer-depends on `@nestjs/common` â‰¤10; for Nest 11 you need `@nestjs/config@^4.0.0`. Initial `npm install` failed `ERESOLVE`; bumped to `^4.0.0` and resolution succeeded.</result>
